title: Pro tier = literally unlimited unmetered Anthropic spend, and Pro is free to mint
mechanism: checkAndConsume() short-circuits `if (unlimited) return {allowed:true, remaining:null}` for any tier != free, so Pro/trial devices are NEVER counted against any quota. The ONLY server-side spend guard is the free-tier 15-drafts/week counter; Pro drafts (interactive suggest, autodraft, threadIntel, insight, dossier, ask, voicePersona — all 7 sites share this one wire) have zero server ceiling. There is no global Anthropic budget or circuit-breaker anywhere. And 'Pro' is trivially forgeable: validateLicenseKey() returns valid Pro for ANY key starting 'OSMO-', which flips subscriptionActive=true → resolveTier returns 'pro' → unlimited.
trigger: Anyone POSTs /api/license/validate {licenseKey:'OSMO-anything'} to become Pro, then hammers /api/suggest (directly or via a scripted client) with no per-request cap and no aggregate cap.
breaksAt: Immediately for a single motivated actor; also organically — a legit heavy Pro user driving per-thread threadIntel + 30/day autodraft + ad-hoc ask/dossier can run the Anthropic bill unbounded with no server backstop.

title: Client-chosen `model` forwarded to Anthropic verbatim — force the most expensive model per call
mechanism: The suggest route reads `body.model` (default 'claude-sonnet-5') and passes it straight into the Anthropic request body with no allowlist/validation. A caller can substitute the most expensive available model (e.g. an Opus tier) and multiply per-request cost several-fold on EVERY call. Combined with the missing global budget and the unauthed path below, this turns each burned request into a maximally expensive one.
trigger: Any client (the real app, a modified app, or a raw HTTP caller) sets `model` in the POST body to an expensive model id.
breaksAt: Every request — the multiplier applies immediately and stacks on top of any volume-based runaway.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:46 (model = body.model ?? default), :79 (model passed to anthropicBody unvalidated)

title: With ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set but OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH!=1, anyone burns the key uncapped and unmetered
mechanism: Auth is only enforced when OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH==='1'. Otherwise the route skips the 401 gate entirely. Quota is only applied when a device is resolvable from the bearer (`device = bearer ? deviceByToken : null`); with no/garbage bearer, device is null, so checkAndConsume is NEVER called — the request goes straight to Anthropic with a real key, unmetered. So the single config slip of not setting OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH turns /api/suggest into an open, uncapped Anthropic relay for the whole internet.
trigger: Production has ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set (real bill) but OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH is unset/!=1; an attacker POSTs /api/suggest with any systemCore+userTurn and no valid device token.
breaksAt: As soon as the key is added without the auth flag — the confirmed default posture is 'keyless dev, auth optional', so this is the natural first production state.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:31-36 (auth only if OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH==1), :61-63 (device null when no valid bearer => quota block skipped), :88-96 (real Anthropic call)

title: NEW plan's background quality gate is a 2–4x LLM multiplier with no stated budget
mechanism: The proposed S4→S5 background path turns one draft into generate-2 (2 full generations) + a Haiku judge call + a conditional S5 repair generation = up to ~4 LLM calls per delivered draft. Layered on autodraft firing per inbound (up to 30/thread-day) and threadIntel per thread per inbound, the background tier multiplies the exact volume that today has no aggregate ceiling. The plan gates it to 'background only' and makes repair conditional, but specifies no global spend cap or per-account background budget — so the multiplier rides on top of the same uncapped Pro wire.
trigger: Every backgrounded draft (which, with autodraft-on-arrival, is most inbound-triggered drafts for a Pro user) fans out to 2 generations + judge + maybe repair.
breaksAt: At the scale the plan is designed for — routine inbound volume for an active Pro user — background spend is 2–4x the already-uncapped foreground spend.
evidence: PLAN S4/S5 (generate-2 -> Haiku judge -> conditional repair); today's single-call wire it multiplies: App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:844-878 (autodraft per inbound), :1110-1128 (threadIntel per thread)

title: Person enrichment has no result cache; 2 paid upstreams guarded only by a per-process, per-device window
mechanism: enrichPerson() calls Unipile getUserProfile AND Exa searchPerson (numResults 5) on every request with NO result cache — re-enriching the same person re-hits both paid APIs. The only guard is a module-level Map of 10 requests/60s KEYED PER DEVICE. Because /api/device/register is open and unlimited, one actor mints N devices to get N×(10/60s) enrichment budget. The map is in-memory on one process, so a redeploy resets it, and it provides zero cross-request dedup for identical names. Worse, exa.ts retries 3x with backoff on 429/5xx, so a flaky/rate-limited Exa turns each admitted request into up to 3 paid Exa calls.
trigger: A person-page-flipping spree, a bot minting devices, or simply an upstream returning 429/5xx (triggering the 3x retry) — each admitted enrich = Unipile call + up to 3 Exa calls, uncached.
breaksAt: Sustained browsing or any multi-device/scripted actor; the per-device 10/60s cap is not a spend cap and is defeated by open device minting.
evidence: web/lib/enrich/person.ts:25-59 (no cache, Unipile+Exa each call); web/app/api/enrich/person/route.ts:12-19,38 (per-device per-process window); web/lib/enrich/exa.ts:18-35 (3x retry)

title: /api/connect/rebackfill has no rate limit and no in-flight guard — repeated/concurrent runs re-page paid Unipile history
mechanism: rebackfill sets the connection to 'backfilling' then fires backfillConnection fire-and-forget with no rate limit and no dedup on (deviceId,accountId). Each run pages up to MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES=200 message pages (250/page) plus listChats plus up to 60 listChatAttendees calls — all paid Unipile calls. The only stop is the loop bailing when status != 'backfilling', which does NOT prevent two concurrent rebackfills (both set 'backfilling' and both loop) or a rebackfill overlapping the initial notify-triggered backfill. Ingest dedup prevents duplicate ROWS but does nothing to prevent duplicate paid FETCHES.
trigger: An authed device (registration is open/unlimited) POSTs /api/connect/rebackfill repeatedly or concurrently for a connected platform.
breaksAt: Any scripted loop over the endpoint; each call ≈ up to ~260 paid Unipile requests, unbounded in aggregate.
evidence: web/app/api/connect/rebackfill/route.ts:22-27 (no rate limit, void backfill); web/lib/connections/backfill.ts:18 (MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES 200), :77-81 (paging loop / status-only bail), :64 (listChatAttendees), :59 (MAX_ATTENDEE_FETCHES 60)

title: The 30/day autodraft cap lives only in the Mac client's UserDefaults — no server enforcement for Pro
mechanism: AutodraftPolicy.dailyCap=30 is a pure client-side guard whose counter is persisted in the app's UserDefaults/autodraftCap.json. The server has no autodraft-specific cap, and for Pro the shared quota is unlimited. So deleting/rolling the local cap file, a client bug, or a modified client removes the only limiter, and each autodraft is a full Sonnet suggest call fired on inbound arrival.
trigger: A tampered/buggy client, or simply clearing the local cap state; every inbound to a Pro human 1:1 thread then triggers an unmetered suggest call.
breaksAt: Any Pro user whose local cap state is bypassed — server sees ordinary unlimited Pro suggest traffic and does not distinguish or bound autodraft.
evidence: Sources/OsmoShell/AutodraftPolicy.swift:16 (dailyCap 30, pure/client), App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:827-836 (cap in UserDefaults/JSON), :853-874 (decision then service.suggest)

title: cache_control:ephemeral on every systemCore pays the cache-write premium even for low-reuse call sites
mechanism: The route unconditionally marks the client-sent systemCore as cache_control ephemeral. Anthropic prompt-cache writes cost ~1.25x base input tokens and reads ~0.1x, with a short (5-min) TTL. Each of the 7 call sites composes a DIFFERENT systemCore (suggest/threadIntel/dossier/ask/insight/voicePersona), so infrequent sites (dossier, ask, voicePersona) rarely get a within-TTL read hit — they pay the 25% write premium on the large core with little/no reuse benefit, a net cost increase versus not caching. Any accidental volatile content in systemCore also forces a full-price miss every time.
trigger: Low-frequency call sites, or any drift that puts per-request-varying text into systemCore instead of userTurn.
breaksAt: Steadily on the long tail of infrequent call sites; magnitude scales with how large each site's systemCore is and how sparse its traffic.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:81-84 (unconditional ephemeral cache on systemCore); 7 distinct client-composed cores e.g. Sources/OsmoBrain/ThreadIntel.swift:42, Sources/OsmoBrain/Dossier.swift:51

title: In-memory oplog + dedup map grow unboundedly with full message content on ONE 512MB instance → OOM is the first thing to fall over
mechanism: appendRows() is strictly append-only: it pushes full WireContact/WireThread/WireMessage row objects (including message TEXT) onto oplogs.get(deviceId) and never truncates, compacts, or evicts (memoryStore.ts:206-229). A parallel dedup Map holds a sha1 hex per native key (memoryStore.ts:124,214-215), so every device carries TWO in-heap copies of its entire history's key space. Nothing frees this except account deletion (purgeDevice, :292). Backfill imports up to MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES 200 x 250/page = ~50k messages per account (backfill.ts:17-18) at OSMO_BACKFILL_SCOPE=full/60d (render.yaml:28), across gmail+slack+x+linkedin+whatsapp+instagram per device. All of this stays resident on the single Render 'starter' instance (render.yaml:13, ~512MB). Node heap fills and the process is OOM-killed.
trigger: Users connect accounts and run full-scope backfills; heap accumulates every device's entire retained history with message bodies plus a second sha1 copy, and is never released.
breaksAt: Low hundreds of active devices with real multi-platform histories, or even one power user with a large WhatsApp/Gmail account, exhausts 512MB. OOM -> Render restart -> mass re-register/re-backfill -> re-hash everything -> faster re-OOM.

title: pull() does two O(n) synchronous scans of the whole oplog per request on a single 0.5-vCPU event loop; SSE doorbells make pulls frequent
mechanism: pull() runs oplog.filter(e => e.seq > since) over the ENTIRE array then a second oplog.some(e => e.seq > last) for hasMore (memoryStore.ts:231-247) with no index on seq and no binary search on the sorted array. This is synchronous CPU on Node's single thread. Every sync.dirty doorbell (published once per backfill page, per send, per inbound) plus the app's reconciliation timer triggers a /api/sync/pull, and each pull re-scans a possibly-50k-entry array. appendRows also hashes every row via JSON.stringify+sha1 synchronously (:144-145,214). Because the instance is 0.5 vCPU and single-threaded, concurrent large pulls + concurrent backfill hashing serialize on one event loop; latency for ALL routes (SSE heartbeats, /api/suggest, /api/health) balloons behind them.
trigger: Many devices with large oplogs pulling concurrently while backfills hash rows; each doorbell fans a device into a full-array rescan.
breaksAt: Tens of concurrent pulls against multi-thousand-entry oplogs, or a backfill spike, saturates the single vCPU; Render's health check can then time out -> restart -> stampede.

title: SSE doorbell fanout is a per-process globalThis map with a per-connection setInterval — it hard-caps the system at ONE instance and leaks timers on abrupt disconnects
mechanism: Subscribers live in a globalThis Map<deviceId,Set<Controller>> (events.ts:10-17); publish() only reaches controllers in THIS process (:26-34). Because the oplog seq counter and the doorbell publisher are both per-process, a second instance is architecturally impossible: a sync.dirty published on instance A never reaches a device's SSE stream pinned to instance B, so you cannot horizontally scale to relieve the OOM/CPU pressure above — the only lever is a bigger single box, which has a ceiling. Every device also holds ONE always-open SSE connection continuously (not just while drafting), each with a 25s setInterval heartbeat (events.ts:56-59). The interval is cleared ONLY in cancel() (:61-65); if the client TCP drops without a clean cancel (common behind proxies/laptop sleep), the controller stays in the Set with its heartbeat interval firing forever — a slow timer+socket leak that compounds until a publish() happens to hit it and throw.
trigger: Every active user holds a persistent stream; scaling users linearly grows persistent connections + live timers on one process, and you cannot add instances to shed load.
breaksAt: Thousands of concurrent devices = thousands of persistent sockets + timers on one 0.5-vCPU/512MB box; and the moment you try to add a 2nd Render instance to cope, doorbells silently stop delivering to half of users.

title: All 7 LLM sites funnel through one blocking, non-streaming, uncapped /api/suggest wire with NO global concurrency or spend cap; the plan's background quality gate 3-4x's the fanout
mechanism: /api/suggest issues a single blocking fetch to Anthropic with no timeout, no retry, no streaming, max_tokens 700 (suggest/route.ts:78-100). There is NO concurrency limiter, semaphore, queue, or global spend circuit-breaker anywhere in web/ (grep confirms none). Each in-flight suggest holds a Node request open for the full multi-second Anthropic latency on the single instance. The demand multiplier is large: threadIntel fires exactly one Claude call per thread per new inbound (ThreadIntel.swift:167-173) and autodraft pre-computes up to dailyCap=30 drafts/day/device (AutodraftPolicy.swift:17) on inbound. Under load Anthropic org RPM/TPM/concurrency limits return 429/529 -> the proxy returns 502 with NO retry (:98-99) -> the Swift client has no retry and GeneratorRouter silently swallows the error and returns MOCK drafts (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:79-86), so at scale users silently get '[mock]' text believing it is real. The NEW BACKEND plan's S4 generate-2 -> Haiku judge -> S5 repair adds 3-4 more calls per backgrounded draft, multiplying the very fanout that has no cap.
trigger: Inbound-message volume x per-thread threadIntel x per-device autodraft precompute, all sharing one uncapped wire; a marketing spike or a busy-inbox cohort.
breaksAt: Aggregate LLM calls exceed the Anthropic org concurrency/TPM cap or the (nonexistent) budget; users start silently getting mock output and cost scales linearly with inbound volume with no ceiling.

title: Backfills run as unbounded fire-and-forget promises INSIDE the web process, competing with request serving and lost (not resumed) on every redeploy
mechanism: connect/notify kicks off backfillConnection with `void ...` — no await, no queue, no concurrency limit (connect/notify/route.ts:47). The loop runs inside the same Node process serving pulls/SSE/suggest, doing up to 200 sequential Unipile page fetches + up to 60 attendee fetches, normalizing and sha1-hashing thousands of rows into the in-heap oplog (backfill.ts:76-146). N users connecting near-simultaneously (a launch/marketing spike) spawn N concurrent backfill loops on ONE 0.5-vCPU instance with no admission control, saturating the event loop and Unipile rate limits at once and inflating heap toward OOM. Worse, it is pure in-memory in-process work: autoDeploy:true redeploys on every push to main (render.yaml:17), and any redeploy or OOM mid-backfill discards all progress; the connection is left 'backfilling'/'degraded' (:148-160) and the retry restarts the ENTIRE 50k-row re-page from scratch — amplification, not resume.
trigger: Concurrent account connections (onboarding wave) or any redeploy/restart during in-flight imports.
breaksAt: Dozens of simultaneous first-time connects, or a single deploy during a backfill wave, saturates CPU/Unipile and forces full re-imports for every interrupted device.

title: Device auth reads ONLY in-memory state, so every restart 401s ALL devices → synchronized re-register stampede that mints new IDs, hammering Supabase and orphaning device rows forever
mechanism: requireDevice() validates the bearer token against getStore().deviceByToken — the in-memory map only (auth.ts:14-22); the durable copy written to Supabase osmo_devices at registration is NEVER consulted for auth. So any restart (OOM, or autoDeploy on any push to main, render.yaml:17) wipes the in-memory devices map and EVERY active device's next request 401s. The Swift client responds by re-registering (BackendClient.swift:330-334 -> register 61-73), and registerDevice() mints a brand-new dev-UUID + token every call (memoryStore.ts:160-168). This produces three compounding scaling problems: (1) a synchronized thundering-herd of /api/device/register + re-pull + SSE-reopen across the whole active base on every deploy; (2) each register does a Supabase upsert with a NEW id (device/register/route.ts:13), so osmo_devices accumulates one orphaned row per device per restart, growing without bound; (3) the old device-owned subscription/trial (keyed to the discarded deviceId) is orphaned, resetting free-tier state. This turns any single failure into a base-wide recovery event that itself drives load toward the next failure.
trigger: Any process restart — frequent given autoDeploy:true on every commit, plus OOM/CPU-timeout restarts from the findings above.
breaksAt: At 10x users every deploy = 10x simultaneous register writes to Supabase + 10x simultaneous full re-pulls/SSE reconnects; osmo_devices row count grows by (active devices) per deploy indefinitely.

title: Magic-link verifyUrl is returned in the HTTP response body unconditionally (even with Resend configured) with no rate limit -> full web-account takeover for any email
mechanism: POST /api/auth/request mints a valid 15-min single-use magic-link token and, on EVERY response, returns `verifyUrl` (containing the raw token) in the JSON body. The `if (process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)` block is an empty TODO comment that neither sends the email NOR strips the URL — so even in 'live' mode the token is handed straight back to the caller. Anyone POSTs {email:'victim@x.com'} and receives the exact verify link; GET-ing it (/api/auth/verify) does findOrCreateUserByEmail + createWebSession and sets the osmo_session cookie for that user. That session drives /account and /api/account/upgrade. There is no per-email/IP throttle.
trigger: Unauthenticated POST /api/auth/request with a target email; read verifyUrl from the response; open it.
breaksAt: Immediately, for any known email address (emails are public); worse the instant real users exist since it grants their /account portal + subscription controls.
evidence: web/app/api/auth/request/route.ts:22-27 (empty RESEND block; verifyUrl always in body); web/app/api/auth/verify/route.ts:18-23 (consumes token -> session cookie)

title: Every paywall bypass is LIVE in production because Stripe is intentionally omitted, so all `if (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY)` guards evaluate false
mechanism: render.yaml deliberately ships NO Stripe key ('Stripe intentionally omitted — launching free-first'). The 'disabled once a real Stripe key is present' safety on the dev/mock grant routes therefore never engages in prod. GET /api/checkout/mock-complete?device=<id> has NO auth and flips subscriptionActive:true for any deviceId that exists in the accounts DB (every registered device); /api/license/reset stays live; /api/account/upgrade takes its mock branch and activates Pro for any web session. /api/checkout/session hands the app a mock-complete URL. Chained with the open /api/device/register, an attacker self-serves unlimited Pro.
trigger: Register a device (open) to get a deviceId, then GET /api/checkout/mock-complete?device=<deviceId>. No auth header needed on mock-complete.
breaksAt: In production now — the very configuration Osmo plans to launch with (no Stripe key) is what leaves these routes armed.
evidence: web/render.yaml:63 (Stripe omitted) + :26-27 (OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1 only); web/app/api/checkout/mock-complete/route.ts:9,19 (gate off in keyless, grants Pro, no auth); web/app/api/account/upgrade/route.ts:16-24

title: Any license key beginning 'OSMO-' is accepted as an active Pro subscription
mechanism: validateLicenseKey() uppercases/trims and returns {valid:true, plan:'com.osmo.pro.monthly'} for anything starting 'OSMO-'. POST /api/license/validate (device Bearer only, obtainable via open register) then setSubscriptionForDevice({subscriptionActive:true}) and returns a server-SIGNED entitlement the app fully trusts. This is independent of Stripe, so it works even after real Stripe keys are added.
trigger: POST /api/license/validate with Bearer <freshly-registered device token> and body {licenseKey:'OSMO-anything'}.
breaksAt: Immediately and permanently (survives adding Stripe), affecting all revenue.

title: /api/account/link performs NO Apple JWT verification and trusts a client-supplied email, enabling subscription theft and account hijack from a victim's email alone
mechanism: The route trusts body.appleUserID and body.email verbatim. findOrCreateUserByApple: when no user matches the (attacker-invented) appleUserID, it falls back to matching the supplied email; if a user row already exists for that email (from a prior Apple sign-in or web magic-link login), it OVERWRITES that user's apple_user_id with the attacker's value and returns the victim's user. account/link then linkDeviceToUser(attackerDevice, victimUser), so subscriptionForDevice(attackerDevice) now resolves to the victim's Pro subscription and returns a signed Pro entitlement. It also repoints the victim's apple_user_id, and the attacker can then POST /api/account/delete (device Bearer) to purge the victim's user, subs, sessions and all their devices.
trigger: Register a device, then POST /api/account/link with that Bearer and {appleUserID:'<any>', email:'<victim email>'}.
breaksAt: As soon as a victim has an account row (any Apple sign-in or web login). Emails are effectively public, so blast radius = every account.
evidence: web/app/api/account/link/route.ts:15-31 (no Apple token verify); web/lib/accounts/store.ts:271-275 (email branch overwrites apple_user_id, returns existing user); web/app/api/account/delete/route.ts:12-14 (device -> full user purge)

title: Unauthenticated, unthrottled /api/device/register + per-DEVICE quota/trial = infinite fresh trials and unlimited Anthropic spend
mechanism: POST /api/device/register mints a device token with no auth, no rate limit, no proof-of-work. The free-tier weekly quota (checkAndConsume) and the 14-day trial (startTrialForDevice) are both keyed to deviceId, and /api/suggest identifies the payer by device token. So an attacker loops register -> startTrial (or just register per 15 drafts) to mint unbounded 'trial'/free identities, each granting unmetered or reset LLM access. This directly attacks the crown-jewel Anthropic bill, which has no global spend cap.
trigger: Script POST /api/device/register N times; call /api/trial/start on each; hammer /api/suggest.
breaksAt: At any scale an attacker chooses; each device = a new 14-day unlimited trial or a fresh 15/week bucket.
evidence: web/app/api/device/register/route.ts:9-19 (open, unlimited); web/lib/license/quota.ts:28-38 (keyed per deviceId); web/app/api/trial/start/route.ts:8-12

title: /api/suggest trusts client systemCore verbatim and an unvalidated model id, with auth reduced to mere token-presence
mechanism: In prod (OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1) auth only checks that the bearer maps to SOME registered device — trivially satisfied by open register. body.systemCore is forwarded verbatim as the Anthropic cache_control system block (the named prompt-injection / safety-bypass hole: no server Safety re-run), and body.model is passed straight to Anthropic with no allow-list, so a caller can select an arbitrarily expensive model on Osmo's key. Keyless mode returns a mock and bypasses the quota path entirely.
trigger: POST /api/suggest with a valid device token, an attacker-authored systemCore, and model:'<expensive-model-id>'.
breaksAt: Immediately; systemCore hole is the plan's headline MUST-FIX, but the model-id trust and presence-only auth are separate live gaps.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:30-49 (presence-only auth, unvalidated model default), :78-86 (model + systemCore forwarded verbatim, no Safety re-run)

title: Long-lived device token accepted via ?token= query for ALL device-authed endpoints, leaking a full credential into logs/referrers
mechanism: requireDevice() falls back to reading the token from the URL query string for every route that uses it — not just SSE. Because the same helper backs POST/DELETE routes (account/delete, sync/send, license/validate, media, etc.), a caller can authenticate any destructive action via ?token=. Query strings are captured by Render access logs, upstream proxies, browser history and Referer headers, so a single leaked URL yields replayable full device auth (the token is a long-lived Keychain secret, not a short-lived session).
trigger: Any request of the form /api/account/delete?token=<deviceToken>; or capture such a URL from logs/referrer.
breaksAt: Whenever a token-bearing URL is logged or forwarded — routine in production behind Render + CDNs.
evidence: web/lib/connections/auth.ts:16-18 (query-string token fallback, used by every requireDevice route)

title: Committed dev Ed25519 signing keypair is the fallback signer -> forgeable Pro entitlements if the env key is ever unset
mechanism: sign.ts hardcodes DEV_PRIVATE_D/DEV_PUBLIC_X and privateKeyObject() uses them whenever OSMO_LICENSE_PRIVATE_D/OSMO_LICENSE_PUBLIC_X are absent. The app bundles the matching public key to verify entitlements offline; if the shipped app bundles the DEV public key (or the server boots without the secret env, which render.yaml marks sync:false = not guaranteed set), anyone with the committed private key can forge a Pro/trial SignedEntitlement the app accepts with no server contact. A single misconfigured deploy silently downgrades the entire trust root to a public key.
trigger: Server started without OSMO_LICENSE_PRIVATE_D set, or app bundling DEV_PUBLIC_X; forge payload {tier:'pro'} and sign with the committed d.
breaksAt: On any deploy/env-drift where the signing secret is missing; offline entitlement verification means it is undetectable server-side.
evidence: web/lib/license/sign.ts:17-18 (committed keypair), :38-42 (env-or-DEV fallback used for real signing); web/render.yaml:37-40 (key only sync:false, not guaranteed)

title: Stripe webhook does no signature verification AND writes to an ephemeral store that the entitlement path never reads
mechanism: Two compounding bugs: (1) With STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET set, the handler still JSON.parses the raw body directly (constructEvent is a TODO), so any unauthenticated caller can forge checkout.session.completed / subscription.deleted for any client_reference_id. (2) It writes via getStore().setLicense() into the in-memory connections store, but /api/license/validate resolves tier from the SEPARATE durable accounts store (subscriptionForDevice) — the two are disconnected, so real Stripe payments would never grant Pro and forged events flip a value nothing reads. Net: billing is both spoofable and non-functional.
trigger: POST /api/webhooks/stripe with a forged JSON event body once a webhook secret exists.
breaksAt: The moment Stripe is wired for real revenue; today it silently no-ops.
evidence: web/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts:15-33 (JSON.parse, no constructEvent, writes getStore().setLicense); vs web/app/api/license/validate/route.ts:22 reading getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice

title: Inbound webhooks lack cryptographic signatures; the shared secret is optional and travels in the query string
mechanism: /api/webhooks/unipile and /api/connect/notify verify only an OPTIONAL shared secret (OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET); if unset, both are fully open. connect/notify additionally accepts the secret via ?secret= in the URL (set on notify_url), so it is logged by Unipile, Render, and proxies. With the secret unset or leaked, an attacker who learns/guesses a connection id can inject fabricated inbound messages into a victim device's oplog (published to its SSE + pulled as real messages) or force a connection to 'degraded'. OAuth-backed connection ids (gmail-/x-<deviceId suffix>) are partially derived from the deviceId, narrowing the guess.
trigger: POST /api/webhooks/unipile with {account_id:<conn id>, event:'message', ...} when OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset; or replay a leaked ?secret= notify URL.
breaksAt: Any deploy where OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET (sync:false) is not set, or once a notify URL with ?secret= is captured from logs.
evidence: web/app/api/webhooks/unipile/route.ts:10-13 (optional secret, no HMAC); web/app/api/connect/notify/route.ts:15-20 (?secret= query); web/lib/unipile/real.ts:50-52 (secret embedded in notify_url query)

title: Send blocks on the full provider round-trip with no optimistic local echo and no server-side fetch timeout
mechanism: POST /api/sync/send awaits the provider send (Gmail/Slack/X/Unipile) and only records the oplog row AFTER the provider returns the real platformMessageID (appendRows on line 62 is after the awaited send on lines 35-43). This is a deliberate 'no temp IDs' design (route header lines 1-4). So the drafted reply the user just tapped Send on does not appear anywhere until the entire upstream round-trip completes. The server-side fetch() calls in send.ts have NO timeout, so a TCP-connected-but-silent provider hangs the request indefinitely; the client (BackendClient.urlSessionTransport uses URLSession.shared.data, default 60s request timeout) gives up at 60s and throws, but the send may have actually succeeded upstream. Gmail is doubly slow: sendGmail makes TWO sequential upstream calls (thread-metadata GET on line 47, then messages/send POST on line 74) inside the one blocking request.
trigger: User taps Send on a drafted reply while the provider is slow, rate-limited, or degraded (or Gmail at all).
breaksAt: Any provider p95 spike; guaranteed felt on Gmail (2 serial round-trips). A hung provider hangs the request up to the client's 60s ceiling with no local echo the whole time.

title: The crown-jewel interactive draft is fully blocking and non-streaming — user stares at a spinner for the whole generation with zero feedback
mechanism: /api/suggest calls Anthropic with a plain (non-streaming) fetch and returns only after the full completion is decoded (route.ts lines 88-108, max_tokens 700, no stream:true). The Swift client (ClaudeProxyGenerator) posts via URLSession.shared.data with NO custom timeout, so the interactive suggest call blocks up to URLSession's 60s default with no partial tokens, no progress, no first-token latency win. Prompt caching cuts cost but not wall-clock for generated output tokens; because the psychology core is a large cached system block, latency is dominated by output token generation that the user experiences as one long silent wait.
trigger: Every interactive draft request (the primary user action).
breaksAt: Felt on every call; painful whenever Sonnet output latency is multi-second, and any tail-latency event blocks with no feedback up to 60s.

title: No retries on the LLM wire, and the client falls back to mock ONLY on URLError — a transient Anthropic 529/overload becomes a hard user-facing failure
mechanism: The proxy does zero retries and maps any non-2xx Anthropic response to a 502 (suggest/route.ts lines 98-100). The Swift ClaudeProxyGenerator throws GenerationError.http(502) for non-2xx (line 57). GeneratorRouter.generate catches ONLY GenerationError.notConfigured and URLError (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift lines 82-84) — a GenerationError.http(502) is NOT caught, so it propagates as a hard failure to SuggestionService.suggest. So Anthropic's common transient 429/529 (overloaded) turns straight into a failed draft with no retry and no mock fallback, even though the router was designed to degrade gracefully. All 7 call sites (suggest/judge/threadIntel/insight/dossier/ask/voicePersona) share this one wire and inherit the gap.
trigger: Anthropic returns 429/503/529 (overload/rate-limit) or the proxy returns 502 for any reason.
breaksAt: Manifests under Anthropic load spikes (529s are routine at scale) and any brief upstream blip — no retry means single-shot failure.

title: Backfills are fire-and-forget promises with no durable queue/resume; the 'reconciliation poller' that supposedly retries them does not exist server-side
mechanism: Every backfill is launched as an un-awaited `void backfill*(...)` inside a request process (connect/notify:47, rebackfill:26, accounts:48 on resume, and all 3 oauth callbacks). backfill.ts:22 and :153 claim 'the reconciliation poller retries', but grep shows NO server-side timer ever re-invokes backfillConnection — the only 'reconciliation timer' is the CLIENT's cursor-pull (events.ts:3-4), which just re-pulls existing rows and cannot resume a dead import. So a redeploy/crash/promise-rejection mid-import kills the in-flight backfill and leaves the connection pinned 'backfilling' or 'degraded' forever; the user waits indefinitely for history that never lands until they manually hit /api/connect/rebackfill. Since backfill state and the oplog are in-memory on one instance, a Render redeploy wipes both mid-import.
trigger: Redeploy, process crash, or any thrown rejection during a fresh connection's history import.
breaksAt: Every deploy that overlaps an in-flight backfill; guaranteed on first-connect for large accounts because the import runs tens of seconds.

title: Gmail backfill does up to 300 SEQUENTIAL full-message fetches and appends to the oplog ONCE at the very end — nothing lands for the whole import and a restart loses all of it
mechanism: gmailBackfill accumulates every message/contact/thread in local Maps and calls store.appendRows/publish exactly ONCE after the loop (lines ~232-238), unlike the Unipile backfill which appends per page (backfill.ts:120). The loop does one sequential `messages/{id}?format=full` fetch per message up to MAX_MESSAGES=300 (gmailBackfill.ts:13,182-183), each a serial round-trip. So the user sees zero messages and gets zero sync.dirty doorbells for the entire import, then everything appears at once — and if the single instance restarts or the promise throws before the final append, the whole import is discarded with nothing persisted and no resume. 300 serial fetches also monopolize the single instance's outbound for the duration.
trigger: Any Gmail connection (first connect or rebackfill), especially a busy mailbox hitting the 300-message ceiling.
breaksAt: Every Gmail import of non-trivial size; total loss on any restart within the multi-second window (which for Gmail is the whole import).

title: Background LLM traffic (threadIntel per inbound + up to 30 autodrafts/day) shares the one blocking wire, Anthropic account, and single instance with the interactive draft — no priority lane
mechanism: threadIntel (one full Sonnet call per thread per new inbound, ThreadIntel.swift:167-173) and autodraft precompute (up to dailyCap=30/day, AutodraftPolicy.swift:16) all go through the same GeneratorRouter -> /api/suggest wire as the user's interactive draft. There is no separate endpoint, model tier, queue, or rate budget separating background from interactive traffic, and Render pins ONE instance. A burst of inbound (backfill completion firing many threadIntels, or a morning email dump) fires N concurrent full-model calls that contend with the interactive draft the user is actively waiting on for the shared Anthropic account concurrency/rate limit — the interactive call the user blocks on can queue behind background work or trip a shared 429.
trigger: Inbound burst (post-backfill fan-out, morning inbox fill, or many autodrafts) coinciding with a user opening a thread to draft.
breaksAt: Manifests once inbound volume x threadIntel + autodraft approaches the Anthropic account's per-minute rate/concurrency limit; a single active user post-backfill can trigger it.

title: Enrichment blocks the user on two paid upstreams fetched sequentially with no cache; repeated lookups re-fetch and hit a per-process 10/60s wall
mechanism: enrichPerson awaits Unipile getUserProfile FIRST (person.ts:42) then Exa searchPerson (line 55) — sequential, not Promise.all — so the dossier open blocks on the sum of both round-trips, with no server-side fetch timeout. There is no result cache anywhere in web/lib/enrich, so flipping back to the same person re-fetches both paid upstreams every time; the only guard is a per-process sliding window of 10/60s (enrich/person/route.ts:12-23) which, once tripped, 429s the user's dossier open. On a restart the window map is wiped (in-memory), and on the single instance the window is global to the process.
trigger: User opens/refreshes a person dossier; or flips through more than 10 people in a minute.
breaksAt: Every cold dossier open pays serial upstream latency; the 11th lookup in 60s hard-fails with 429.

title: Media proxy fully buffers Gmail/Unipile attachments (no streaming) and enforces the 25MB cap AFTER allocating the buffer — double latency plus OOM risk on the one instance
mechanism: For Gmail the route does res.json() to pull the entire base64 payload into memory, Buffer.from(...) decodes the whole file, and only then binaryResponse checks byteLength > MAX_BYTES (media/route.ts:64-66,29-30) — so an over-cap file is fully downloaded and decoded before rejection. The Unipile path also fully buffers (downloadAttachment returns bytes, line 100). Only Slack streams via res.body passthrough (lines 90-95). The user therefore waits for the full server-side download + decode + re-upload (serialized, not streamed) for Gmail/Unipile media, and concurrent large-attachment requests each hold the whole file in RAM on the single starter instance, risking OOM.
trigger: Opening any Gmail/Unipile attachment, especially large ones; several at once.
breaksAt: Latency felt on any sizeable Gmail/Unipile attachment; memory pressure/OOM when a few near-cap fetches overlap on one instance.

title: LLM message-derivatives (dossiers/insights/voice) + attachment bytes are stored PLAINTEXT outside SQLCipher and SURVIVE "Erase all data"
mechanism: The whole privacy story is 'encrypted on your Mac' (SQLCipher DB keyed by KeychainDBKey). But the app writes a fleet of message-DERIVED artifacts as plaintext JSON in Application Support, bypassing the encrypted store entirely: dossiers.json (AppModel.swift:1429-1438), insights.json (AppModel.swift:1442-1451), voicePersona.json (AppModel.swift:1521-1531), plus account.json/connections.json/cursors.json/autodraftCap.json; and downloaded attachment bytes land in a plaintext dir at Application Support/Osmo/media (MediaStore.swift:16-20). deleteAllData() (AppModel.swift:1844-1849) only calls store.deleteAllData() (DB rows, OsmoStore+Shell.swift:240-254), clears the device token, and unsets hasOnboarded. It removes NONE of those files and never calls a MediaStore clear. So the distilled relationship intelligence (arguably more sensitive than raw rows) and the attachment media remain readable on disk after the user hits ERASE, after account deletion, and after a Mac is sold/returned. exportJSON() (OsmoStore+Shell.swift:210-235) also omits dossiers/insights/voicePersona/media, so 'Export all data' is simultaneously INCOMPLETE.
trigger: User views Dossier/Insights/You(voice) or receives any attachment, then later uses Settings > Erase all data / Delete account, or disposes of the machine.
breaksAt: Manifests for essentially every engaged user; realized as a privacy/compliance breach on device resale, forensic access, or a GDPR/CCPA erasure/portability request.
evidence: App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:1429-1531 (dossiers/insights/voicePersona plaintext writes), MediaStore.swift:16-20, AppModel.swift:1844-1849 (deleteAllData) + OsmoStore+Shell.swift:240-254 (exportJSON/deleteAllData scope)

title: Voice/dossier/insight calls egress VERBATIM private sent-messages to Claude; the 'keep none / never train' proof cannot cover Anthropic-side retention and the model is client-chosen
mechanism: PERSONALIZATION isn't just 'pseudonymous scalars': VoicePersona.compose ships up to 20 verbatim user sent-messages as 'SAMPLE LINES' (VoiceStats.swift:156-159) and ensureVoicePersona feeds outboundMessages(limit:60) — 60 raw sent messages — into the proxy (AppModel.swift:1506-1510). The proxy forwards them to api.anthropic.com with NO retention control: no ZDR/no-train header, no org/DPA assertion, and the MODEL is taken from client body.model unvalidated (suggest/route.ts:46,78-96). The stated privacy proof is a 'log-scrubbing test' — but that can only prove the PROXY stores nothing; it is structurally incapable of proving Anthropic's upstream 30-day abuse-retention isn't holding these messages, and a client passing an arbitrary model string sidesteps any model-scoped ZDR the org might have. The claim 'we keep none, never train' is thus asserted about a third party the code neither configures nor can enforce.
trigger: Any real (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set) drafting / voicePersona / dossier / insight / threadIntel call — i.e., normal Pro usage.
breaksAt: Live from the first keyed request; becomes a broken-promise/legal exposure the instant a user, auditor, or regulator asks to see the ZDR+DPA behind the marketing sentence.

title: Signed config registry introduces a THIRD app-bundled Ed25519 key whose compromise is a silent fleet-wide push — and which itself can only be rotated by the OTA it exists to avoid
mechanism: The system already has two long-lived Ed25519 domains with no rotation story: the entitlement key (public half hard-baked in the binary, EntitlementVerifier.swift:34; payload field 'v' is a schema version, NOT a key id; committed DEV private fallback in sign.ts:15-18) and the Sparkle EdDSA key (Info.plist:39). The plan adds a THIRD: a signed config registry carrying directives/thresholds/PER-TASK MODEL IDS/kill-switch flags (today the flags route is entirely UNSIGNED, flags/route.ts). Its verifying public key must be byte-stable and app-bundled, so the one key that can push new prompts, swap model ids (cost/safety), or flip aiDrafting for ALL users instantly — with NO Apple notarization/Gatekeeper in the loop that a real OTA has — cannot be rotated or revoked without shipping the very OTA the registry was built to eliminate. There is no kid, no rotation, no revocation, no expiry in any of the three domains. A leak of the config private key is a silent, un-recallable, fleet-wide control-plane takeover strictly worse than a bad OTA.
trigger: Config-signing key leak/loss, or need to rotate after staff departure; also any attacker who can serve the registry response if verification has any gap.
breaksAt: At the moment rotation is first needed, or on first key compromise; blast radius = entire installed base, applied without user consent or OS gatekeeping.

title: Built-but-dormant E2EE blob sync flatly contradicts 'messages never leave your Mac', and its static-salt HKDF makes 'server can't read' false for the whole userbase the moment it's enabled
mechanism: SyncEngine + BlobStore ship a complete off-device oplog sync; the real store is 'a thin Supabase/S3 client added with credentials last' (BlobStore.swift:12-14). Enabling it sends the encrypted message oplog to Supabase/S3 — directly contradicting the headline claim AND the plan's 'only de-identified pseudonymous scalars leave device.' Worse, the encryption that supposedly saves the claim is broken as designed: CryptoBox.key() uses HKDF-SHA256 (NOT a memory-hard KDF) over a passphrase with a SINGLE hardcoded GLOBAL salt 'osmo.static.salt.replace.in.prod' (CryptoBox.swift:32-39). Static global salt ⇒ identical passphrase yields the identical key for EVERY user and enables one precomputation attack amortized across the entire userbase; HKDF (no Argon2id) makes offline passphrase brute-force cheap. So 'the server only ever stores opaque ciphertext it can never read' collapses to trivially-crackable the day sync is switched on.
trigger: Wiring a real BlobStore (the explicitly-planned 'added last' step) and turning on cloud/multi-device sync.
breaksAt: First real deployment of sync; brute-force feasibility is worst for any low-entropy/shared passphrase and is population-wide because the salt is global.

title: SyncEngine push/pull cursors aren't persisted → every relaunch re-appends ALL local changes (unbounded duplicate-ciphertext growth) and re-decrypts the entire remote log
mechanism: SyncEngine holds lastPushedSeq/pullCursor in memory only, defaulting to 0, with the doc admitting 'the app persists them across launches (a small follow-up — noted, not blocking)' (SyncEngine.swift:5-21). Because the BlobStore is append-only, on every relaunch push() re-exports and re-appends the ENTIRE local change set as fresh ciphertext blobs (BlobStore has no dedup — BlobStore.swift:30-38), so the server log grows without bound proportional to launch count, and pull() with pullCursor=0 re-fetches, re-decrypts, and re-applies the whole log each launch. This is a storage-cost and battery/CPU sink that compounds silently, and it undermines any DR reasoning about the remote log size.
trigger: Any app relaunch once a real BlobStore is connected.
breaksAt: Grows linearly per relaunch per device; becomes a cost/performance problem for heavy users within days of enabling sync.

title: The SQLCipher passphrase exists in exactly ONE un-escrowed local file — its loss = permanent, unrecoverable loss of the entire message DB; no backup/DR
mechanism: KeychainDBKey.loadOrCreate() generates a 256-bit key once and writes it to a single 0600 local file via LocalSecretStore (KeychainDBKey.swift:15-30, deliberately NOT the macOS Keychain), and OsmoDatabase.open AES-encrypts the whole DB with it (OsmoDatabase.swift:18-25). There is no escrow, no export, no recovery phrase, and Time Machine/any backup captures only ciphertext that becomes undecryptable if that key file is lost or corrupted (Migration Assistant skipping it, disk corruption, or the app's own token/keychain-clear paths). Because everything hinges on one un-backed-up secret, a routine OS event silently converts the user's entire relationship history into unrecoverable bytes — a DR hole the 'it's all safely on your Mac' framing hides.
trigger: Loss/corruption of the local key file, Mac migration that drops it, or restoring a Time Machine DB onto a machine without the original key.
breaksAt: Any single key-file loss; unrecoverable by construction (no key = no data, and no second copy exists).

title: The golden-set eval harness and the log-scrubbing privacy test — the ONLY proofs behind the quality-ceiling and 'keep none' claims — do not exist in the repo
mechanism: Phase 3 'golden-set eval' and Phase 0's 'log-scrubbing test' are cited as the falsifiable backing for the floor-gate/ceiling quality guarantees and the privacy claim. A repo-wide search finds no golden-set, eval harness, or scrub/redaction test (only vendor/GRDB false positives). So the quality bar ('S4 generate-2 → Haiku judge → S5 repair produces better drafts', 'evidence-gated ceiling') and the privacy assertion are today entirely unfalsifiable — there is no CI gate that would catch a prompt/config change (now shippable WITHOUT OTA via the registry) that silently regresses draft quality or starts leaking PII into logs.
trigger: Any prompt/model/config iteration (especially via the un-OTA'd signed registry) with no eval or scrub gate to catch regressions.
breaksAt: Immediately — the guarantees are asserted now while the harness proving them is unbuilt; a bad config push regresses quality/privacy with nothing to catch it.
evidence: No matches for golden/eval/scrub/redact harness under Tests/ or web/tests/ (grep returns only vendor GRDB); web/app/api/config/flags/route.ts (config path that would need the gate)

title: Three hand-maintained version-of-truth sources; /api/version already reports an OLDER build than what is shipped
mechanism: App update truth is the Sparkle appcast (Info.plist SUFeedURL → leftonread.in/appcast.xml, currently 0.2.1/build 3, appcast.xml). But there are two other independently hand-edited version sources: the build config (project.yml:44-45, 0.2.1/3) and the backend stub /api/version (version/route.ts:3-8) which STILL returns 0.2.0/build 2 — already stale below the shipped build. Nothing couples app build ↔ appcast ↔ backend version, so any consumer that trusts /api/version (a website 'latest', a future force-update/min-version gate) will misreport or mis-gate, and the drift is invisible because no test asserts the three agree.
trigger: Any release; or introducing a min-version/force-update check that reads /api/version.
breaksAt: Already diverged today; bites when a force-update or 'latest version' feature is wired to the stale endpoint.

title: Backend outage silently swapped for client 'mock' text; autodraft persists literal "[mock] ..." as a ready-to-send draft
mechanism: GeneratorRouter.generate catches `URLError` and returns MockGenerator output, which is the literal string "[mock] on <their message>: here's the direct reply..." (Generator.swift:24-33). Any backend-unreachable condition (Render down, DNS, TLS, client timeout at the URLSession 60s default) surfaces as a URLError, so the router treats a real production outage identically to 'dev proxy not running' and returns mock prose. The interactive SuggestionStrip at least post-hoc detects `text.contains("[mock]")` and shows a DEMO badge (SuggestionStrip.swift:139), but the BACKGROUND autodraft path (considerAutodraft, AppModel.swift:868-876) does NOT: it takes `result?.set.takes.first?.text`, sees non-empty mock prose, and calls `store.saveDraft(text, forThread:, isAuto: true)` with no mock filter. The queue then presents "[mock] on ...: the lighter reply 🙂" as the auto-drafted reply. isMockMode (BackendClient.swift:76-78) is decided from register().mode == "mock" (keyless-ness), NOT reachability — a healthy production backend that is merely DOWN yields register throwing -> `(try?...)?.mode` == nil -> false, so autodraft's `!isMockMode` guard passes during the outage and the mock draft is written.
trigger: Any transient network/backend unavailability during a background autodraft-on-arrival (fired on every inbound message).
breaksAt: First inbound message during any Render outage or user network blip while the session's cached isMockMode is false; one-tap send from the queue then delivers 'mock' garbage to a real contact.

title: OAuth access tokens never refreshed: Gmail send dies ~1h after connect, X ~2h — refresh tokens are obtained then discarded
mechanism: sendGmail/sendX use the access_token captured at connect time and stored in-memory (send.ts:41,10; x/callback/route.ts:33 stores the whole bundle but the code only ever reads .access_token). Gmail access tokens expire ~1h, X ~2h. `refreshXToken` exists (providers.ts:124) but grep confirms it is never called anywhere, and there is NO Gmail/Slack refresh call at all — even though the auth URLs request refresh grants (Gmail access_type=offline+prompt=consent providers.ts:54-56; X offline.access providers.ts:43). After the token TTL, the provider returns 401, sendGmail/sendX throw, and /api/sync/send maps it to 422 'send rejected' (sync/send/route.ts:44-48). The connection is not even marked degraded on a send failure, so the UI shows 'connected' while every reply silently 422s until the user manually disconnects/reconnects.
trigger: Sending any Gmail reply >~1h (or X DM >~2h) after connecting the account.
breaksAt: Universally, ~1h/~2h after each connect; the app's core promise (draft + send a reply) is broken for Gmail/X within an hour of onboarding.
evidence: web/lib/oauth/providers.ts:124 (refreshXToken never called); web/lib/oauth/send.ts:10,41; web/app/api/oauth/x/callback/route.ts:33; web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts:44-48

title: No send idempotency key -> provider-succeeds-but-response-lost causes duplicate real messages to the recipient
mechanism: SendRequest carries only {platform, platformThreadID, text} — no idempotency key (types.ts:149). /api/sync/send calls the provider then mints a WireMessage with the provider-returned messageId (sync/send/route.ts:50-59). If the provider actually delivers the message but the HTTP response back to the server is lost (network reset, client-side URLSession 60s timeout on a slow Gmail send, Node undici bodyTimeout), the server's fetch throws and returns 422; the app shows 'send failed' and the user re-taps send. The oplog dedups on platformMessageID (comment sync/send/route.ts:1-4), but the SECOND provider send produces a NEW platformMessageID, so dedup never fires — the counterpart receives two copies of an intimate message. sendGmail also never checks res.ok on its thread-metadata lookup (send.ts:47-53), so a mid-flight token expiry there builds a malformed empty-recipient reply, compounding partial-send confusion.
trigger: A slow/interrupted provider send where the send lands but the response is lost, followed by a user retry.
breaksAt: Any flaky-network send; probability rises with large Gmail threads (metadata fetch + send round-trips push past client timeout).

title: Free-tier quota is consumed BEFORE the Anthropic call and never rolled back on failure; an Anthropic incident drains a user's weekly quota producing zero drafts
mechanism: In /api/suggest, checkAndConsume() bumps the weekly usage counter (suggest/route.ts:69) BEFORE the Anthropic fetch (line 88). If Anthropic returns non-2xx the route returns 502 (lines 98-100) with the quota already decremented and no rollback; there is no retry/backoff on the wire. During an Anthropic 429/529 overloaded incident (common and transient), each interactive draft / regenerate tap burns one of the free user's 15/week while returning an error. A short Anthropic blip can therefore exhaust a paying-adjacent free user's entire weekly allowance without delivering a single usable draft.
trigger: Any Anthropic upstream error (429/500/529/timeout) while a free-tier device is drafting.
breaksAt: Free tier during any Anthropic degradation; a handful of retries on a flaky moment silently eats the 15/week cap.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:63-76 (consume) then :88-100 (fetch, 502 on failure, no rollback/retry); web/lib/license/quota.ts:28-38

title: Process restart mid-backfill = total connection + half-imported-history loss; the promised 'reconciliation poller' does not exist for OAuth providers
mechanism: backfillGmail/Slack/X and backfillConnection are fired as unawaited `void` promises inside the OAuth-callback request process (google/slack/x callbacks; accounts/route.ts:48). backfill.ts:22 claims 'failures leave the connection backfilling and the reconciliation poller retries', but the only restart-heal is adoptOrphanedAccounts, which is (a) gated behind OSMO_ADOPT_ORPHANS=1, off by default, and (b) Unipile-live-only — it never re-runs Gmail/Slack/X OAuth backfills (accounts/route.ts:24-30). Since connections, OAuth tokens, and the per-device oplog are all in-memory, a Render redeploy/restart mid-import wipes them; the app then gets 401 -> re-registers -> cursor reset -> re-pulls from seq 0 into an EMPTY oplog. The half-imported history is gone and the connection object no longer exists, so the user must re-OAuth every provider — on every deploy. The `degraded` state the catch blocks set is itself in-memory and evaporates on the same restart.
trigger: Any server restart/redeploy (or an in-request crash) while a first-connect import is running.
breaksAt: Every Render redeploy that overlaps an active backfill; on a frequently-restarting single instance, users are forced to reconnect providers repeatedly.

title: Gmail/Slack/X have no inbound path after the one-shot backfill — every new message is a permanently 'missed webhook'
mechanism: The OAuth backfills are one-shot imports (gmailBackfill: a single messages.list over `newer_than:Nd`, no history.list/users.watch Pub/Sub; slackBackfill: conversations.history once; xBackfill: once). There is NO Gmail push watch, NO Slack Events subscription, NO X polling, and no cron/worker (render.yaml has no cron; instrumentation.ts only ensures Unipile hooks). The only live inbound path is /api/webhooks/unipile, which matches connectionById(account_id) and only ever fires for Unipile-managed platforms (LinkedIn/WhatsApp/Instagram) — it never delivers Gmail/Slack/X messages. Consequently, after the initial import the app's own email/DM channels silently stop updating: new inbound Gmail/Slack/X messages never append to the oplog, so autodraft-on-arrival and threadIntel never fire for them and the inbox looks frozen for those providers until a manual rebackfill.
trigger: Any inbound Gmail/Slack/X message arriving after the initial connect import completes.
breaksAt: Immediately post-backfill for every OAuth provider; 100% of subsequent messages on those channels are missed.
evidence: web/lib/oauth/gmailBackfill.ts:147-249 (one-shot, no watch); web/app/api/webhooks/unipile/route.ts:19-31 (connectionById match, Unipile-only); render.yaml (no cron); web/instrumentation.ts

title: Supabase sits on the critical path of every paid draft with unhandled failure: a Supabase blip silently downgrades Pro users to free (429) or 500s all drafting
mechanism: /api/suggest awaits getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice(device.id) with no try/catch, no timeout, no cache (suggest/route.ts:67) purely to decide the quota tier — drafting itself needs nothing from Supabase. The Supabase implementation destructures only `{ data }` and ignores the `error` field (store.ts:291-293), so a Supabase query error resolves to `mapSub(null) -> freeSub`. Result: while Supabase is degraded, a Pro/trial user is silently treated as FREE, hits checkAndConsume, and is 429'd 'quota_exceeded' after 15 drafts/week despite paying. If instead the underlying fetch hard-rejects (network fully down), the un-try/catch'd await throws and Next returns 500, taking ALL drafting offline. Either failure mode is a silent wrong-answer coupling drafting availability to an unrelated dependency.
trigger: Any Supabase outage/latency spike (or its fetch rejecting) during a draft request from an authed device.
breaksAt: Pro/trial users during any Supabase degradation get throttled to free; a hard reject 500s the whole draft path.

title: No timeout/circuit-breaker on the server Anthropic fetch; one slow-Anthropic incident can exhaust the single always-on instance shared by all 7 LLM call sites
mechanism: The proxy's fetch to Anthropic has no AbortSignal/timeout (suggest/route.ts:88-96) and Node undici defaults leave headers/body timeouts high (~300s), so a hung Anthropic request holds a server request open far longer than the client's 60s URLSession default. The client times out at 60s -> URLError -> falls to mock, but the server socket lingers. All 7 LLM call sites (suggest/judge/threadIntel/insight/dossier/ask/voicePersona) funnel through this one blocking, non-streaming wire onto ONE render-pinned instance with no global spend cap or circuit breaker; threadIntel fires one call per new inbound and autodraft up to 30/day, so during an Anthropic slowdown a burst of long-lived hung requests can saturate the single instance's concurrency and stall unrelated API routes (sync/pull, accounts, SSE) that share the process.
trigger: Anthropic latency spike / partial outage while inbound-driven threadIntel + autodraft keep issuing calls.
breaksAt: Single-instance concurrency ceiling during any sustained Anthropic slowdown; the whole backend (not just drafting) degrades because state and LLM share one process.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:88-96 (no signal/timeout, no retry); Sources/OsmoBrain/ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:33-37 (no request timeout); render.yaml (one instance)

title: Auth validates the device token against ephemeral memory, so every redeploy orphans the durable device + its subscription (split-brain between memoryStore.devices and accounts.osmo_devices)
mechanism: requireDevice() resolves the bearer token ONLY via getStore().deviceByToken() — the in-memory MemoryConnectionsStore.devices map (token -> Device). The durable AccountsStore (Supabase osmo_devices) DOES persist the same {id, token} (upsertDevice writes token), but the AccountsStore interface exposes only deviceById — there is NO deviceByToken, so auth can never fall back to the durable copy. After any restart/redeploy the memory devices map is empty, so EVERY authenticated request 401s. The Swift client's authed() treats 401 as 'backend forgot me': it dropCredentials() (clears the Keychain token), then POSTs /api/device/register, which ALWAYS mints a brand-new dev-<uuid> + token (there is no re-register-as-existing-id path). The client now runs under a NEW deviceId whose durable subscription row does not exist (owner_type=device, owner_id=oldDeviceId is orphaned; owner_type=user requires a manual /api/account/link re-run). The old device row lingers forever; every redeploy leaks another orphan.
trigger: Any Render restart, redeploy, crash, or OOM (single always-on instance, all device auth state in-memory).
breaksAt: Every user on every deploy — Render redeploys on each push; the one pinned instance has no shared/durable token store.

title: Stripe cancellation is a lost write to a disconnected store, so cancelled Pro never lapses and the 7-day offline grace never counts down
mechanism: customer.subscription.deleted in /api/webhooks/stripe calls getStore().setLicense(deviceId,{subscriptionActive:false}) — writing to the EPHEMERAL memoryStore.licenses map. But /api/license/validate resolves tier from getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice() — the DURABLE Supabase osmo_subscriptions table. These are two different stores; nothing ever writes subscriptionActive=false into the durable store on a billing event. So the durable record stays active forever. Because buildSignedEntitlement re-issues expiresAt = now + OFFLINE_GRACE_DAYS(7) on every validate, and the client re-validates online periodically, the offline-grace window is perpetually refreshed off a stale 'active' — the grace clock never starts ticking down after cancellation. Compounded: the webhook also no-ops unless store.deviceById(deviceId) is truthy, which after any restart it is not (memory devices empty), so even the wrong-store write is frequently dropped.
trigger: A user cancels their subscription (Stripe emits customer.subscription.deleted), or a payment lapses.
breaksAt: Every paying user who cancels — revenue leak is permanent, not time-bounded; and manifests the moment real Stripe is wired.

title: Rejected identity merges resurrect: merge_decision is device-local (never synced) AND its pairKey is unstable under cluster membership change
mechanism: Two independent defects. (1) Cross-device: rejectMergePair writes to the merge_decision table, which is absent from OsmoStore.syncTables (only contact/person/thread/project/relationship_memory/message sync) and carries no SyncMeta columns — so a rejection recorded on device A never propagates. Device B, which DOES receive the synced person/contact rows, re-runs rebuildIdentityGraph and re-surfaces the exact pair the user already dismissed on A, forever. (2) Single-device: pairKey(a,b) is built from each cluster's MIN contact UUID, and resolve() reconstructs the lookup key from the CURRENT cluster's min (reps[i]). When a cluster gains a member sharing a global key (e.g. connecting WhatsApp adds a same-phone contact whose UUID is smaller) or loses its current-min member (tombstoned via the isDeleted filter, or flagged isMe), the min changes, the reconstructed key no longer equals the stored key, and the rejected suggestion reappears. The code comment claiming 'same phone/email => same contacts => same min => survives rebuilds' is false whenever cluster membership changes.
trigger: (1) A second Mac / any device-to-device sync. (2) Connecting a new platform (or deleting/merging a contact) that alters a previously-reviewed cluster's membership.
breaksAt: Multi-device users always (cross-device); single-device users the first time a rejected cluster's min contact changes — common as more platforms are connected.

title: Deterministic Person IDs are minted from a cluster's MIN contact UUID, so two devices converging out-of-order create two Person rows for one real person (identity split-brain under LWW)
mechanism: rebuildIdentityGraph mints a new Person id = DeterministicID.v5('person:' + cluster.min(uuidString)). Contact IDs are deterministic (v5 of platform+handle) so a real person's contacts share UUIDs across devices — BUT the MIN of the cluster depends on WHICH contacts a device has ingested at rebuild time. During initial sync convergence, device A may see {c1,c2} (min c1 -> personID=v5(person:c1)) while device B, mid-pull, sees only {c2} (min c2 -> personID=v5(person:c2)). Each links its contacts to a different personID, stamps deviceSeq/updatedAt, and syncs. applyChanges is last-writer-wins by updatedAt: both Person rows now exist on both devices, and each contact's personID is whichever device wrote it later — so the single real person is split across two Person records. RelationshipMemory and Project rows (keyed by personID) split with it; the enrichment (keyed by personID) orphans. rebuildIdentityGraph's 'reuse existingPID = members.first' only pins the FIRST assignment per device; it cannot reconcile two devices that each assigned first.
trigger: Two devices (Sign in with Apple + device-to-device sync) ingesting the same person's contacts in different order / at different times.
breaksAt: Any multi-device account during the initial backfill window, or whenever a contact arrives on one device before the other.

title: A server redeploy instantly drops paying users to Free (defeating the 7-day offline grace) because re-registration mints a new deviceId that fails the entitlement's device binding
mechanism: The signed entitlement is bound to a specific deviceId (payload.deviceId), and EntitlementVerifier.verify returns nil whenever expectedDeviceID != payload.deviceId. When a redeploy forces re-registration (see device-identity finding), the client's expectedDeviceID becomes the NEW dev-<uuid>, so its cached, still-time-valid Pro/trial entitlement fails the device-mismatch check and verify() returns nil -> the app treats itself as Free IMMEDIATELY. The whole point of OFFLINE_GRACE_DAYS=7 (keep Pro through an offline trip / brief outage) is nullified: instead of gracefully honoring the cached signature, a mere backend restart revokes entitlement, and the new deviceId has no durable subscription to re-issue Pro. The user's paid state silently evaporates until (and only if) they manually re-run Sign in with Apple to re-link.
trigger: Any backend restart/redeploy that causes the 401 -> re-register -> new deviceId path while the user holds a valid cached entitlement.
breaksAt: Every Pro/trial user on every deploy; worse than a true outage because the app has a valid signed grace token it refuses to honor under the new id.

title: Client backend cursor only resets on 401/re-register; migrating the per-process oplog to a durable/multi-instance seq space (Phase 0) will silently skip or double-deliver rows
mechanism: The oplog seq is a per-process counter (seqs map, seq += 1 in appendRows) reset to 0 on restart; pull() returns rows with seq > since. The client persists the returned cursor opaquely and resets it to '' ONLY via onReRegistered (i.e. on a 401). The plan's Phase-0 'durable state' aims to keep device tokens valid across restarts — precisely the case where NO 401 fires. If the durable oplog re-numbers seqs (a fresh DB sequence, or a per-device sequence with different values than the pre-migration in-memory counter), an existing client holding cursor=N will call pull(since=N): durable rows minted with seq <= N are never delivered (permanent silent gap), or if the new space starts higher, older content is re-shipped. Because the only cursor-reset trigger is re-registration, and durable auth deliberately avoids re-registration, cursors are never invalidated at the cutover. Multi-instance amplifies it: each instance's per-process counter diverges, so the same device pulling from instance A then B sees non-monotonic seqs and misses rows.
trigger: Cutover from the in-memory oplog to a durable (or multi-instance) oplog while devices hold pre-migration cursors; or scaling past one instance.
breaksAt: At the Phase-0/Phase-2 migration and at any horizontal scale-out (both explicit goals of the redesign).

title: Enrichment cache keyed on the unstable personID and never synced: identity re-clustering orphans it (paid re-fetch) and every device re-fetches independently
mechanism: PersonEnrichment is keyed solely by personID, is explicitly device-local (absent from syncTables, cascades with its person), and is considered fresh for 7 days (isStale). Two consequences. (1) When the identity graph re-mints a personID (the min-UUID split-brain above) or a manual merge tombstones the old person, enrichment(forPerson:) for the new/surviving id returns nil, forcing a fresh fan-out to the two PAID upstreams (Unipile + Exa), while the old row lingers as garbage. (2) Because it is device-local, each device independently fetches and independently ages its own 7-day window; there is no shared/server cache today. The plan's proposed server enrichment cache (Phase 5) would sit in front of a client cache that keys on a personID the server cannot reproduce (personID is a device-local identity-graph artifact, not derivable from a stable global key), so the two caches key on different identifiers and diverge / double-spend rather than dedup.
trigger: Identity re-cluster or merge changing a personID; or the same person viewed on a second device; or a proposed server cache layered over the client's personID-keyed cache.
breaksAt: Any account with identity churn or >1 device; cost scales with paid Unipile+Exa calls (guarded only by a per-process 10/60s window, no cache).

title: The server-enforced free-tier bill guard lives in a different (ephemeral) store from the tier and is keyed on the volatile deviceId, so it resets to zero on every redeploy or re-register
mechanism: checkAndConsume reads/writes the weekly counter via store.usage/bumpUsage — the in-memory MemoryConnectionsStore.usage map — while the tier that decides 'unlimited' is read from the DURABLE accounts subscription (subscriptionForDevice). The counter is keyed per-deviceId. Because (a) the usage map is wiped on every restart and (b) the deviceId itself is re-minted on every 401 re-register (device-identity finding), a free user gets a fresh 15-draft allowance after any redeploy or by simply provoking re-registration — defeating the stated purpose ('the guard a lying client can't bypass'). The counter (ephemeral) and the tier (durable) also diverge in lifetime, so a device that was capped can be simultaneously 'over quota' per the durable tier's expectation yet counted at zero. Note: within a single Node process the read-modify-write itself is not racy — bumpUsage is synchronous with no await inside — so the real defect is cross-store durability + volatile keying, not concurrent double-spend.
trigger: Any restart/redeploy, or a client that forces re-registration, between quota consumption and the weekly reset.
breaksAt: Every free user, every deploy — Anthropic spend has no durable per-account ceiling backing it.
